source_paper_title: Finding Hall Blockers by Matrix Scaling
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Hayashi_2024, title={Finding Hall Blockers by Matrix Scaling}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0198}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0198}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Hayashi, Koyo and Hirai, Hiroshi and Sakabe, Keiya}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2166–2179} }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (discussion of operator scaling extension and problem raised in prior work)

It is a natural question whether the operator Sinkhorn algorithm can find a Hall blocker for nonscalable case. This may need to characterize the limits of the operator Sinkhorn algorithm, which is raised as a problem in [18].
title: Characterize and compute limits of operator Sinkhorn algorithm for nonscalable operators
background_latex:
Let \(\Phi\) be a completely positive linear operator acting on complex matrices (equivalently, a linear map \(\Phi: \mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) that maps positive semidefinite matrices to positive semidefinite matrices). Operator scaling asks for invertible matrices \(L,R\) such that the scaled operator \(X\mapsto L\,\Phi(RXR^*)\,L^*\) satisfies prescribed left and right marginals (the operator analogue of making a nonnegative matrix doubly stochastic). The operator Sinkhorn algorithm (also called the Gurvits algorithm) is an iterative normalization procedure that alternately rescales the operator to correct its left and right marginals, generalizing the classical Sinkhorn algorithm for matrix scaling.

In the nonscalable case (i.e., when no choice of \(L,R\) can make \(\Phi\) doubly stochastic, even approximately), an analogue of a Hall-type certificate exists: a nontrivial subspace \(W\subsetneq \mathbb{C}^n\) that is "shrunk" by \(\Phi\) (often called a shrunk subspace). Existence of such a shrunk subspace certifies nonscalability, in the same way that a Hall blocker certifies absence of a perfect matching for bipartite graphs.

The cited paper discusses extending matrix-scaling-based certificate-finding results to operator scaling, and notes that doing so may require understanding the limiting behavior (or limit objects/accumulation structure) of the operator Sinkhorn iterates in the nonscalable case.
problem_definition_latex: Determine (i.e., characterize and provide a method to compute) the limits or accumulation-point structure of the iterates produced by the operator Sinkhorn algorithm when applied to a completely positive operator \(\Phi\) that is not (approximately) scalable to doubly stochastic form.
latex:
Let \(\Phi\) be a completely positive linear operator acting on complex matrices (equivalently, a linear map \(\Phi: \mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) that maps positive semidefinite matrices to positive semidefinite matrices). Operator scaling asks for invertible matrices \(L,R\) such that the scaled operator \(X\mapsto L\,\Phi(RXR^*)\,L^*\) satisfies prescribed left and right marginals (the operator analogue of making a nonnegative matrix doubly stochastic). The operator Sinkhorn algorithm (also called the Gurvits algorithm) is an iterative normalization procedure that alternately rescales the operator to correct its left and right marginals, generalizing the classical Sinkhorn algorithm for matrix scaling.

In the nonscalable case (i.e., when no choice of \(L,R\) can make \(\Phi\) doubly stochastic, even approximately), an analogue of a Hall-type certificate exists: a nontrivial subspace \(W\subsetneq \mathbb{C}^n\) that is "shrunk" by \(\Phi\) (often called a shrunk subspace). Existence of such a shrunk subspace certifies nonscalability, in the same way that a Hall blocker certifies absence of a perfect matching for bipartite graphs.

The cited paper discusses extending matrix-scaling-based certificate-finding results to operator scaling, and notes that doing so may require understanding the limiting behavior (or limit objects/accumulation structure) of the operator Sinkhorn iterates in the nonscalable case.

Determine (i.e., characterize and provide a method to compute) the limits or accumulation-point structure of the iterates produced by the operator Sinkhorn algorithm when applied to a completely positive operator \(\Phi\) that is not (approximately) scalable to doubly stochastic form.
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (discussion of operator scaling extension and problem raised in prior work)
open_problem_quote: It is a natural question whether the operator Sinkhorn algorithm can find a Hall blocker for nonscalable case. This may need to characterize the limits of the operator Sinkhorn algorithm, which is raised as a problem in [18].
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for a general characterization of limiting behavior for all nonscalable completely positive operators, not a bounded finite-family statement where a single small instance would refute a conjecture. While one can simulate iterates numerically, a counterexample would not resolve the characterization question without a matching theory framework.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically represent completely positive operators (e.g., via Kraus operators) and simulate operator Sinkhorn iterations, using empirical convergence/oscillation diagnostics as a fitness signal to hypothesize structural patterns. However, translating discovered patterns into a correct general characterization is nontrivial and likely requires substantial theoretical work beyond automated search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the matrix case, Hayashi–Hirai–Sakabe analyze nonscalable Sinkhorn iterates by reinterpreting the algorithm as alternating minimization for KL-divergence between the row- and column-marginal affine slices. When the target marginals are infeasible, the iterates converge to an oscillating pair \((M^*,N^*)\) minimizing the KL objective, and the limit marginals \(p^*=N^*\mathbf 1\) admit an explicit block-constant form governed by a refined Dulmage–Mendelsohn (DM) decomposition / principal partition of a polymatroid. This yields a concrete “limit object” (a canonical flag/partition) from which Hall blockers can be read off by sorting the limiting marginals.

For operator scaling, the forward-citation literature provides substantial partial progress toward an analogous description, but not a complete characterization of the accumulation set of the standard operator Sinkhorn (Gurvits) iteration in the nonscalable regime. The paper “Gradient descent for unbounded convex functions on Hadamard manifolds and its applications to scaling problems” develops a general recession-function theory for geodesically convex objectives on Hadamard manifolds and applies it to the operator-scaling Kempf–Ness potential. In the nonscalable case, it proves that gradient flow/descent diverges but converges in cone topology to a unique boundary direction characterized by a generalized DM-flag and the minimum-norm point of the moment polytope; this direction canonically encodes shrunk-subspace certificates. Complementarily, “A scaling characterization of nc-rank via unbounded gradient flow” gives a duality between minimal achievable scaling residuals and the recession function \(f^\infty\), identifying \(f^\infty\) with a Lovász-extension-type formula over flags/subspaces, again linking limit directions at infinity to shrunk subspaces.

On the algorithmic side, “Shrunk subspaces via operator Sinkhorn iteration” shows that one can deterministically compute the (unique) minimum shrunk subspace in polynomial time using a Sinkhorn-style iteration for a modified (majorization/perturbed) capacity, together with rounding from approximate independent sets. This effectively computes a canonical certificate associated with nonscalability, but it bypasses a full dynamical description of the original operator Sinkhorn iterates’ accumulation points. Overall, the problem of characterizing and computing the actual limit/accumulation structure of the unmodified operator Sinkhorn algorithm for nonscalable completely positive operators appears to remain open; the most promising direction is to transfer the recession-function/DM-flag machinery from continuous-time gradient flows to the discrete alternating-normalization dynamics, aiming for an operator analogue of the matrix-case oscillatory limit pair and its block-triangular structure.

sources.title: Gradient descent for unbounded convex functions on Hadamard manifolds and its applications to scaling problems
sources.theorem_or_result: For the operator-scaling Kempf–Ness function \(f_A(x,y)=\log\operatorname{tr}\sum_{\ell=1}^N xA_\ell yA_\ell^\dagger\) on the Hadamard manifold \(\mathcal P_n^1\times\mathcal P_m^1\), if \(A\) is not \((1/n,1/m)\)-scalable then gradient descent/flow diverges to infinity but converges in cone topology to the unique minimizer of the recession function \(f_A^\infty\) (Theorems 3.7 and 4.24, Corollary 4.25). The limit direction is explicitly characterized by a generalized Dulmage–Mendelsohn (DM) decomposition: there is a canonical coarse DM-flag \(\mathbb C^n=X_0\supset\cdots\supset X_\theta=\{0\}\), \(\{0\}=Y_0\subset\cdots\subset Y_\theta=\mathbb C^m\) yielding block sizes \((n_\alpha,m_\alpha)\) and a vector \((p^*,q^*)\in\Delta_A\) (minimum-norm point of the moment polytope) given by \(p^*=-\tfrac1n\mathbf 1+\tfrac1{C_A}\sum_{\alpha=1}^\theta \tfrac{m_\alpha}{n_\alpha+m_\alpha}\mathbf 1_{I_\alpha}\), \(q^*=-\tfrac1m\mathbf 1+\tfrac1{C_A}\sum_{\alpha=1}^\theta \tfrac{n_\alpha}{n_\alpha+m_\alpha}\mathbf 1_{J_\alpha}\), with \(C_A=\sum_{\alpha} \tfrac{n_\alpha m_\alpha}{n_\alpha+m_\alpha}\), and the cone-limit equals \((G^*,H^*)/\|(G^*,H^*)\|\) where \(G^*=(\sigma^*)^\dagger\operatorname{diag}(-p^*)\sigma^*\), \(H^*=(\tau^*)^\dagger\operatorname{diag}(-q^*)\tau^*\) (Theorem 4.24).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize and compute limits/accumulation structure of operator Sinkhorn iterates for nonscalable completely positive operators. This paper does not analyze the alternating-normalization (Sinkhorn/Gurvits) iterates directly, but it gives a detailed characterization of the limit at infinity for closely related first-order methods (gradient flow/descent of the operator-scaling Kempf–Ness function on \(\mathcal P_n^1\times\mathcal P_m^1\)). In the nonscalable case it proves cone-topology convergence to a unique boundary point (a canonical destabilizing direction) and identifies it via a generalized DM decomposition, yielding explicit block-triangular structure and a way to extract a shrunk subspace. It further conjectures an analogous oscillatory limit-set description for the operator Sinkhorn algorithm (end of §4.2), suggesting how the Sinkhorn accumulation might relate to the same DM blocks, but stops short of proving it for Sinkhorn itself.

sources.title: Shrunk subspaces via operator Sinkhorn iteration
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a deterministic polynomial-time Sinkhorn-style method to extract the (unique) minimum shrunk subspace from operator-scaling-type iterations. Concretely, for a CP map \(\Phi\) with Kraus operators spanning a matrix space \(\mathcal A\), it introduces a perturbed/majorization capacity \(\mathrm{cap}_{k,r}(\Phi)=\inf_{X\succeq I,\,Y\succeq I,\,z\ge 0}\big(\mathrm{Tr}(\Phi(X^{-1})Y^{-1}e^{z})+\log\det X+\log\det Y-kz-\tfrac1n\sum_{i=n-r+1}^n\lambda_i(\log X)\big)\) and proves (Theorem 3.10) that \(\mathrm{cap}_{k,r}(\Phi)>-\infty\) iff \((k,r)\le (k^*,r^*)\) in lexicographic order, where \(k^*=\mathrm{nc\text{-}rank}(\mathcal A)\) and \(r^*\) is the minimum \(\dim R\) among maximum independent sets \((L,R)\). It then gives algorithms DecisionSinkhorn/ApproxIndep/RoundSubspaces (Theorems 3.11–3.15) that (i) decide finiteness of \(\mathrm{cap}_{k,r}\), (ii) when \(\mathrm{cap}_{k,r}=-\infty\) output an \(\varepsilon\)-independent set violating \((k,r)\), and (iii) for \((k,r)=(k^*,r^*+1)\) round an exponentially accurate approximate independent set to the exact dominant independent set \((L^*,R^*)\), hence outputting the smallest shrunk subspace \(R^*\), all in deterministic polynomial time over \(\mathbb C\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize and compute the limit/accumulation structure of operator Sinkhorn iterates in the nonscalable case. This paper does not fully characterize all accumulation points of the *standard* operator Sinkhorn (Gurvits) iteration. However, it makes strong algorithmic progress on the core motivation: extracting Hall-type certificates (shrunk subspaces) from Sinkhorn-style iterations when scaling is impossible. It does so by (a) modifying the objective/iteration to a majorization/perturbed capacity whose unboundedness corresponds to existence of a violating independent set/shrunk subspace, and (b) proving that sufficiently long runs yield an approximate shrunk subspace that can be rounded to the exact minimum shrunk subspace. This effectively provides a computable ‘limit object’ (the dominant independent set / minimum shrunk subspace) associated to divergence, even though it bypasses a full dynamical description of accumulation points of the original operator Sinkhorn iterates.

sources.title: A scaling characterization of nc-rank via unbounded gradient flow
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a duality/limit-at-infinity characterization for operator scaling residuals and applies it to nc-rank. In particular, for a matrix tuple \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_m)\) with associated completely positive operator \(T_A(X)=\sum_i A_i X A_i^\dagger\), it shows
- \(n-\mathrm{nc\text{-}rank}(A)=\tfrac12\inf_{g,h\in GL(n,\mathbb C)}\big(\|T_{g^\dagger A h}(I)-I\|_1+\|T^*_{g^\dagger A h}(I)-I\|_1\big)\) (Theorem 1.4).
More generally, for a differentiable geodesically convex \(f\) on \(P_n\) with a \(GL_n\)-invariant Finsler metric induced by a unitarily invariant norm \(\|\cdot\|_v\), it proves
\[\inf_{X\in P_n}\|df(X)\|^*_{v,X}=\sup_{H\in S_n:\ \|H\|_v\le 1}-f^\infty(H),\]
and that along a (metric-space) curve of maximal slope \(X(t)\) one has \(\|df(X(t))\|^*_{v,X(t)}\to\kappa^*\) and any accumulation point of \(H(t):=\log X(t)/d_v(I,X(t))\) attains \(f^\infty(H^*)=-\kappa^*\) (Theorem 3.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize/compute limits or accumulation structure of operator Sinkhorn iterates for nonscalable completely positive operators. This paper does not analyze the discrete operator Sinkhorn (alternating normalization) iterates directly. However, it provides a closely related continuous-time picture: it models operator-scaling residuals as the (dual) norm of the differential of a geodesically convex function \(f\) on \(P_n\), and studies an unbounded gradient-flow/curve-of-maximal-slope \(X(t)\) when \(f\) is unbounded below (the nonscalable regime). It characterizes accumulation points at infinity via the recession function \(f^\infty\), which in the operator-scaling setting is identified with the Lovasz extension of the submodular defect \(U\mapsto \dim(AU)-\dim(U)\) (Lemma 4.4). This gives a structural description of ‘limit directions’ (flags and weights) that correspond to shrunk subspaces/certificates, and a dual optimization problem (Remark 4.6). It is thus partial progress/related framework toward understanding nonscalable limits, but it stops short of proving convergence/accumulation descriptions for the actual operator Sinkhorn iterates or giving an explicit algorithm to compute their limit points.

sources.title: 行列スケーリングから非正曲率空間上の測地的凸最適化へ
sources.theorem_or_result:
This article is an expository survey; it does not prove new theorems about the nonscalable operator Sinkhorn limit. The main formal statements it highlights include Gurvits’ characterization for operator scaling (Theorem 4.1): for a completely positive map 
\[T(X)=\sum_{k=1}^m A_k X A_k^\dagger,\]
approximate doubly-stochastic scalability is equivalent to positivity of the capacity-like infimum 
\[\inf_{X,Y\succ 0}\frac{\big(\sum_k \mathrm{tr}(X A_k Y A_k^\dagger)\big)^n}{\det X\,\det Y}>0,\]
to a Hall-type condition on subspaces \(U,V\) with \(u^\dagger A_k v=0\), and to convergence of operator Sinkhorn when scalable. It also summarizes convex-analytic tools on Hadamard spaces, notably the definition of a recession/‘boundary’ function \(f^\infty\) on the boundary at infinity \(CM_\infty\) (Eq. (5.10)) and the criterion that boundedness of \(f\) implies \(f^\infty\ge 0\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize/compute limits or accumulation points of operator Sinkhorn iterates in the nonscalable case. This survey explicitly notes that in the matrix case, nonscalable Sinkhorn behavior is tied to recession analysis and references work on the nonconvergent case; for operator scaling it discusses the geodesically convex formulation (objective (4.3) on \(\mathcal P_n\times\mathcal P_n\)) and introduces Hadamard-space convex analysis via boundary-at-infinity/recession functions (Section 5.4). These tools are plausibly relevant to describing divergence directions/limit objects of iterates when the objective is unbounded below (nonscalable case), but the article does not itself analyze the operator Sinkhorn iterates’ accumulation structure or provide an algorithm to compute their limits in the nonscalable regime.
